cataloging_subject.label: A Short English Chronicle, from Lambeth MS. 306
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the manuscript-specific sequence copied by one fifteenth-century hand at Lambeth Palace Library MS 306, ff. 1r–46r, printed by James Gairdner on pp. 1–80 of Three Fifteenth-Century Chronicles and encoded as CME00112. Despite Gairdner's collective editorial title and the XML's two DIV2 elements, the sequence contains three intellectual works: an abridged prose Brut, John Lydgate's Verses on the Kings of England copied as prose, and a London civic chronicle for 1189–1465. The record does not flatten those works into one authorial chronicle and keeps Gairdner's notes, the 1880 edition, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, the physical manuscript, and its digital photographs at their proper layers.

catalog_summary.title: A Short English Chronicle, from Lambeth MS. 306
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous abridger and London chronicler; John Lydgate for the Verses on the Kings of England
catalog_summary.date: The three manuscript components were copied circa 1465; the edited sequence was first published in 1880
catalog_summary.region: England, with London specifically associated with the civic chronicle; exact place of compilation unknown

titles.value: A Short English Chronicle, from Lambeth MS. 306

assertion.notes: Gairdner's established editorial heading names the complete printed and encoded sequence, not one medieval witness rubric.

notes: The rubric belongs only to the abridged Brut on ff. 1r–17v, not to the Lydgate verses or London chronicle that follow.

titles.value: In the noble londe of Surrye was some tyme a greate kynge and a myghty that was named Dioclesyan

notes: This is the witness heading above the Lydgate component, not a title for the complete composite.

titles.value: Verses on the Kings of England (to Henry VI)
This myghti William, Duke of Normandie, as bokes olde maketh mencion
The names of the Kepers and Baylyffes of the Cite of London in the tyme of Kynge Richarde the fyrst

agents.name: Anonymous abridger of the Lambeth MS 306 Brut
Anonymous compiler and continuator of the Lambeth MS 306 London chronicle
Lambeth MS 306 Scribe A
notes: The current Lambeth catalog assigns items 2–4 to the same anonymous fifteenth-century Secretary hand. The label identifies a scribal hand, not a named historical person and not necessarily the author of any component.

assertion.notes: The catalog identifies the component as an abbreviated Peculiar Version of the Brut and says scribal errors show that the surviving copy is not an authorial original.
The witness preserves a unique London Chronicle version through 1465; no named author or compiler is supplied.
One hand copies the three components consecutively in the first booklet; this does not prove that the scribe composed or intellectually compiled all three.

date_statements.display: Surviving Lambeth MS 306 copies of all three consecutive components: circa 1465

assertion.notes: The current catalog dates items 2–4 and their common scribal hand circa 1465. This is surviving witness production, not proof that the three intellectual works were first composed or compiled together then. No artificial exact normalized year is assigned to the circa statement.

date_statements.display: Legendary British origins through 1465; the civic chronicle runs from Richard I's reign to Edward IV's fourth regnal year
First published by the Camden Society in 1880

assertion.notes: The directly inspected title page and Roman date M.DCCC.LXXX govern. U-M's 1928 statement and Cambridge Core's 1881 volume date are retained as source discrepancies rather than substituted.

notes: Middle English language and English historical-cultural content support this broad association, but the current catalog does not firmly localize production. This is not an exact place where the composite or its three components were compiled.
The civic chronicle is organized by London keepers, bailiffs, mayors, and sheriffs. The association is not converted into an unsupported place-of-compilation claim.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML declares enm. Gairdner's modern editorial prose and Latin marginal annotations are representation-layer matter and do not become additional work languages.

form_statements.description: Two prose chronicles surround a verse chronicle that the Lambeth witness and XML render as fifteen prose paragraphs.
A generally fifteen-stanza rhyme-royal poem, copied continuously as prose in this witness and encoded as fifteen P elements.

summaries.value: A manuscript-specific historical sequence surviving in copies made circa 1465. It opens with an abbreviated prose Brut running from the legendary daughters of Diocletian and Brutus through the first year of Henry IV. It then copies John Lydgate's Verses on the Kings of England through Henry VI as prose, followed without a new XML division by a London civic chronicle organized under keepers, bailiffs, mayors, and sheriffs from Richard I through Edward IV's fourth year. The final entries report the capture of Henry VI and Edward IV's new coinage. James Gairdner first printed the sequence in 1880.

Gairdner says the sequence looks like one chronicle but is really three short chronicles written or transcribed consecutively by the same pen. The current Lambeth catalog independently describes them as items 2–4: the abbreviated Brut on ff. 1r–17v, Lydgate's Verses on ff. 17v–18v, and the unique London Chronicle version on ff. 18v–46r. Shared folios at both transitions are retained rather than forcing false clean leaf boundaries.

CME00112 contains one DIV1 and only two DIV2 elements. DIV2[1] is the Brut. DIV2[2] joins the fifteen prose-encoded Lydgate paragraphs to the London chronicle beginning at P[16]. Across the XML there are 406 P, 273 NOTE, 134 TABLE, 80 PB, 7 LG, and 19 L elements. The LG and L nodes occur in civic officer-list formatting inside the London chronicle; they are not evidence that the civic chronicle is verse. Gairdner's interleaved notes are modeled as editorial apparatus rather than medieval work content.

The complete source scan's title page dates the Camden Society volume M.DCCC.LXXX (1880), and that date governs this record. The current U-M item says 1928, while the current Cambridge Core section page associates the section with a September 1881 volume date. Those later catalog and platform assertions are retained as unresolved descriptive discrepancies, not silently merged with the inspected publication statement.

extent.description: Four cataloging parts are modeled: three medieval works and Gairdner's interleaved prose apparatus. The medieval sequence consists of two prose chronicles and one fifteen-stanza verse chronicle. Exact verse-line extent is left null because this Lambeth copy rewrites or runs the verse as prose and the XML supplies no recoverable lineation. Raw XML counts are 406 P, 273 NOTE, 134 TABLE, 80 PB, 7 LG, and 19 L; the seven LG and nineteen L elements format civic lists inside the prose London chronicle.

content_parts.label: Cronycullys of Englonde: abridged Brut
content_parts.summary: A unique abbreviated form of the Prose Brut's Peculiar Version, from legendary British origins through the first year of Henry IV.
notes: Folio 17v and IIIF canvas 38 are shared with the following Lydgate component.

content_parts.label: John Lydgate, Verses on the Kings of England (to Henry VI)
content_parts.summary: Fifteen reign stanzas from William I through Henry VI, copied and encoded as prose; DIMEV record 5731 / IMEV-NIMEV 3632.
notes: The witness form and XML element choice are prose, but the intellectual work remains verse.
Folio 18v and IIIF canvas 40 are shared with the London chronicle transition.

content_parts.label: London Chronicle, 1189–1465
content_parts.summary: A unique civic chronicle version arranged by regnal and mayoral years, beginning with keepers and bailiffs under Richard I and ending in Edward IV's fourth year.

locators.value: /ETS/EEBO/TEXT/BODY/DIV1/DIV2[2]/P[16] through the end (295 P, 134 TABLE, 7 LG, 19 L)

notes: The LG and L nodes format civic officer lists and do not change the part's prose form.
Canvas 95 / f. 46r is the confirmed endpoint; canvas 96 / f. 46v begins later John Stow material outside CME00112.

content_parts.label: Gairdner's editorial and manuscript-note apparatus
content_parts.summary: Interleaved footnotes and marginal-note transcriptions identifying readings, corrections, later hands, dates, people, and places.
notes: Some NOTE content reports manuscript marginalia or later corrector interventions, but the encoded NOTE representation belongs to the edition/encoding layer rather than increasing the number of medieval works.

related_works.title: John Lydgate, Verses on the Kings of England (to Henry VI)
notes: The relation is scoped in content_parts to the prose-rendered Lambeth witness version on ff. 17v–18v.

related_works.title: Prose Brut, Peculiar Version to 1400
notes: The relation applies to part:brut-abridgment only; the Lambeth catalog describes it as a unique abbreviated text with no other extant manuscript known.

evidence.locator: IDG, TEXT, BODY/DIV1, both DIV2 elements, PB milestones, NOTE and table/list structures
evidence.summary: Pins CME00112, the Middle English language declaration, printed-page coverage 1–80, exact two-DIV2 encoding, the Lydgate-to-London transition at DIV2[2]/P[16], and raw structural counts.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00112, 'A short English chronicle from Lambeth MS. 306.'
evidence.summary: Confirms the current identifier, public text, inherited title and Gairdner responsibility, U-M's 1928 catalog statement, contents headings, and Public Domain / No Copyright–United States statement.

evidence.citation: James Gairdner, ed., Three Fifteenth-Century Chronicles, Camden Society, new series 28 (Westminster: J. B. Nichols and Sons, 1880), 'A Short English Chronicle, from Lambeth MS. 306,' pp. 1–80.
evidence.locator: Title page and colophon; preface pp. ii–iv; section pp. 1–80 = PDF pages 35–114
evidence.summary: Direct inspection confirms editor, title, publisher, series, the title-page date 1880, the three-component boundary, the circa-Edward-IV manuscript context, the value of the later civic entries, and Gairdner's treatment of the corrupt prose-rendered Lydgate text and later manuscript annotations.

evidence.citation: Lambeth Palace Library Archives Catalogue, MS 306, CHRONICLES, VERSE ETC.
evidence.locator: Composite description; booklet 1; items 2–4; ff. 1r–46r; copies and digital links
evidence.summary: Confirms the current shelfmark, composite codex, ff. i + 204 extent, paper support, dimensions, binding history, John Stow provenance, same-hand circa-1465 copying, three separate items and exact folio loci, PV-1400 classification, unique London Chronicle version, and public digital-facsimile route.

evidence.citation: Direct inspection of Lambeth MS 306 complete IIIF sequence and work boundaries.
evidence.locator: Work ff. 1r–46r = canvases 5–95 / provider page indices 4–94 / bundle images 000005–000095; transitions at canvases 38 and 40
evidence.summary: The complete 415-canvas object and cached bundle confirm the heading and opening on f. 1r, Lydgate and civic transitions on ff. 17v–18v, the chronicle ending on f. 46r, and different later material beginning on f. 46v.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 5731, John Lydgate, Verses on the Kings of England (to Henry VI).
evidence.locator: Work record; Lambeth MS 306 witness 31, ff. 17v–18v
evidence.summary: Confirms authorship, DIMEV and IMEV/NIMEV identifiers, historical-poem and kings-of-England subjects, usual fifteen-stanza rhyme-royal form, Lambeth locus, witness heading and incipit, and the note that this copy is written as prose with a rewritten final stanza.

evidence.citation: Cambridge Core, 'A Short English Chronicle, from Lambeth MS. 306,' Camden New Series, pp. 1–80.
evidence.summary: Confirms the modern section DOI and pp. 1–80 extent while exposing a September 1881 platform-level volume date that differs from the inspected 1880 title page.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for CME00112.
evidence.summary: Provides entity-separated provenance, access, rights, edition, witness, facsimile, catalog, and source-boundary evidence used by this descriptive record.

open_questions.question: Where and under what institutional or civic circumstances did Scribe A copy and join the three circa-1465 components?
next_steps: Consult current codicological and palaeographical scholarship on Lambeth MS 306 booklet 1 and its paper stocks.
Separate evidence for scribal employment, exemplar provenance, compilation place, and authorial activity rather than deriving one from another.
Retain England as broad cultural origin and London as a subject association until direct production evidence supports a narrower place.

open_questions.question: Why do U-M and Cambridge Core describe the publication as 1928 and 1881 respectively when the inspected Camden Society title page is dated 1880?
next_steps: Trace the catalog record and any reissue or volume-title history behind U-M's 1928 assertion.
Check Camden Society distribution records and Cambridge's volume-level metadata for the 1880/1881 difference.
Continue to use 1880 for the inspected edition while preserving the later source assertions as discrepancies.

open_questions.question: How precisely does the Lambeth prose-rendered and partly rewritten copy relate to the fifteen-stanza textual tradition of Lydgate's Verses on the Kings of England?
next_steps: Transcribe ff. 17v–18v directly from the cached facsimile and reconstruct stanza and line boundaries cautiously.
Collate the Lambeth readings against the current DIMEV witness census and a modern critical edition.
Keep verse_lines null until the corrupt prose copy's exact lineation can be defended.

notes: The three medieval works are cataloged separately inside one manuscript-specific composite because both Gairdner and the current Lambeth catalog independently support that boundary.
Shared folios and canvases at ff. 17v and 18v are intentional boundary surfaces, not duplicate or uncertain coverage.

The complete Lambeth facsimile is public to view and cached for private research, but its photographs have no verified open license; this metadata record does not imply permission to redistribute them.
No author, editor, publication date, physical witness, digital provider, or photographic-rights statement is allowed to migrate across layers without explicit evidence.
